image.title: DW
image.description: News, Analysis and Service from Germany and Europe - in 30 Languages

item.title: Dead whale spotted off the coast of Denmark
item.description: It was not immediately clear if the whale was Timmy, the humpback whale that captivated Germany for weeks while getting stranded multiple times off the German coast.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: How international student tuition fees vary across Europe
item.description: A French government plan to charge students from outside the EU 16 times more than previously have stoked discontent. But how does this compare to the rest of Europe, both inside the EU and out?
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Iran says Chinese ships passed through Hormuz overnight
item.description: Chinese-flagged vessels were among over 30 ships that crossed the waterway, according to Iran's revolutionary guard. The statement comes as Trump tries to rally Beijing against Tehran during a state visit.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: China warns Trump over Taiwan question at high-stakes summit
item.description: Chinese state media reported that Xi Jinping raised the question of Taiwan during his meeting with Donald Trump. The pair held talks for about two hours. Follow live.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Ukraine: Over 20 dead as Russian strikes pound Kyiv
item.description: Russia has unleashed a massive volley of missiles and drones at Ukrainian cities for the third day in a row, with the latest strikes hitting a Kyiv apartment block.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Corruption in Ukraine: Will this scandal reach Zelenskyy?
item.description: A drawn-out corruption affair in Ukraine is widening, with authorities now taking action against Andriy Yermak, the former head of the presidential office. Will this scandal reach all the way to President Zelenskyy?
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Cuba's power grid 'critical' as US blocks fuel shipments
item.description: Protests flared in Havana amid widespread power outages. Cuba's energy minister says fuel has "run out" as a four-month US oil blockade chokes off energy supplies.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: UK: Health Secretary Wes Streeting resigns, likely in bid to topple PM Keir Starmer
item.description: Two Labour Party politicians have positioned themselves to challenge Prime Minister Keir Starmer for the leadership. Health Secretary Wes Streeting resigned, and Angela Rayner said her tax affairs were back in order.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Lab-grown Tyrannosaurus leather: More chicken than dinosaur?
item.description: A handbag is promoted as the "world's first T. rex leather product." But scientists are questioning its authenticity.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: German intelligence offices snub US-based Palantir software
item.description: Germany's domestic intelligence agency has reportedly chosen a data analysis system from France, instead of US-based Palantir. Civil society has welcomed the move, but says more must be done.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Who is Mario Draghi, this year's Charlemagne Prize winner?
item.description: The Charlemagne Prize honors those who advance European unity. This year's winner is Italy's Mario Draghi, a former European Central Bank chief and a key architect of European stability during a time of economic crisis.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Angela Merkel rejects 'male-killing' label as 'absurd'
item.description: Germany's first female chancellor, Angela Merkel, has spoken about her rise to the top. She rejected accusations that she ruthlessly pushed aside male rivals as "baseless."
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Iran war tests India's multi-alignment diplomacy
item.description: New Delhi is proud of its carefully balanced ties with rival nations in the Middle East. But this diplomatic strategy might be reaching its breaking point.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: World Cup final halftime show will feature Madonna, Shakira and BTS
item.description: Three major performers from pop, past and present, will perform during halftime of the World Cup final this year. The Superbowl-style pageant will eat into the time to analyze the biggest football game in four years.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Tunisia sliding into authoritarianism, experts say
item.description: The Tunisian government is ramping up pressure on opposition figures, journalists and civil society — raising fresh fears of deepening repression and the steady dismantling of democracy.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Japan: Saving elderly people from 'lonely deaths'
item.description: As Japanese society changes, many elderly people find themselves isolated and facing a "lonely death." Government agencies and private groups are working hard to give them a new lease on life.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Latvia: Prime Minister Evika Silina resigns over handling of drone incidents after coalition ally's withdrawal
item.description: Evika Silina says she is resigning her post, following the defense minister's resignation and the coalition government's collapse amid controversy over Ukrainian drones aimed at Russia that landed in the Baltic country.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Germany news: Merkel urges patience for Merz's coalition
item.description: Former Chancellor Angela Merkel says she thinks people need to have patience with the coalition government, amid widespread dissatisfaction. Meanwhile, Germany's men are celebrating Father's Day.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Iran war tensions, oil shocks dominate BRICS talks in India
item.description: The BRICS bloc faces major challenges as foreign ministers meet in India, bringing Iran and the UAE face-to-face. The conflict in the Gulf and disruptions to oil supplies dominate the agenda.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Honda reports first loss since 1957 as it waters down EV strategy, but shares rise on 2026 forecasts
item.description: The Japanese carmaker pursued an aggressive switch towards electric vehicles that it's now trying to walk back. Despite posting its first loss since 1957, its share price spiked on more buoyant predictions for 2026.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: China goes electric, but can it get off coal?
item.description: The world's clean energy superpower also emits the most CO2. But coal-fired power may have peaked as China electrifies its economy with ever more renewables.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: 'Regime change': Hungary's Magyar exposes Orban's decadence
item.description: Prime Minister Peter Magyar has taken power and laid bare his predecessor's luxurious lifestyle. Many Hungarians are keen to see Magyar chart a new political course.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: US drops bond requirement for FIFA World Cup ticket holders
item.description: Fans from five African World Cup qualifying nations with valid tickets are exempt from paying a hefty bond. But fans from several other countries still face major hurdles to seeing their teams play in the United States.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Germany's shortfall in medium-range defense capabilities
item.description: The US has announced that it will not be deploying Tomahawk cruise missiles in Germany. Now the German government is looking for alternatives. Could drones be part of the solution?
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Fake TikTok fans and pop music's 'unethical' marketing
item.description: Do indie darlings like Sombr and Geese owe their success to TikTok "trend simulations"? The question has sent shockwaves through their fan base. Yet manipulated charts are as old as the music business itself.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Ceasefire in sight? What's next for Russia's war in Ukraine
item.description: Four years since Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine, is the end finally in sight? DW spoke to US political experts about why they think US home elections could bring about a ceasefire.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: China’s new rules give the West a new headache
item.description: Beijing now has strong new powers to punish firms for pulling manufacturing out of China. Multinationals are increasingly caught in a web of coercion and complex rules as they try to operate across the US, EU and China.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Satellites add a new layer to global poverty data
item.description: A new study using satellite data finds that 58% of the global population has been assigned to the wrong tier in the UN's Human Development Index. That can have real-world consequences for how aid reaches people.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: Why men are less worried than women about climate change
item.description: From petro-masculinity to electric trucks: why men disengage from climate action and what might bring them back.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: EU invites Taliban members to discuss Afghan migrant returns
item.description: The European Commission has invited officials from Afghanistan's Taliban to discuss the returns of certain migrants. It's controversial on humanitarian grounds, and because Brussels doesn't recognize Taliban authority.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Hotter 2026 and El Nino could trigger extreme fires
item.description: More than 150 million hectares — over twice the size of Texas — burned globally in the first months of 2026. With a high chance of a supercharged El Nino, the second half of the year could be even worse.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Bundesliga: Kathleen Krüger appointed Hamburg's head of sport
item.description: Hamburg have appointed Kathleen Krüger as the club's new board member responsible for sport. She becomes the first woman to fill such a role at a Bundesliga club on a permanent basis.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Sport helping displaced Lebanese living in stadium to cope
item.description: More than a million people have been forced to flee their homes in southern Lebanon due to fighting between Israeli forces and Hezbollah. As many shelter in Beirut stadiums, sport is sometimes a welcome distraction.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: UK: Keir Starmer tells Cabinet he will continue in place, as four junior ministers quit
item.description: British Prime Minister Keir Starmer has met with his Cabinet amid continued pressure after election losses. A set piece speech yesterday failed to calm the waters, and four junior ministers resigned on Tuesday.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Hijab-wearing German beauty contestant takes on the AfD
item.description: As a Miss Germany contestant, Büsra Sayed wore a hijab on stage. This had never happened before — and the far-right AfD didn't like it. Her response went viral.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: What now for the role of high representative in Bosnia?
item.description: Christian Schmidt's departure as high representative in Bosnia and Herzegovina doesn't look like a routine diplomatic handover. It seems to indicate that international powers want something different from the role.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: EU negotiators agree new rules on vital medicines
item.description: A new agreement aims to tackle the drug shortages that have plagued the bloc. Among other things, it would make it easier to use public funds to support the production of essential medicines.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Ukrainian children held in Russia: militarized, 'reeducated'
item.description: A Ukrainian initiative to repatriate abducted Ukrainian children reports that at least 20,000 girls and boys are being held by Russian families and authorities. Can these children ever find their way home?
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Hantavirus: How is the outbreak being contained?
item.description: Passengers evacuated from the MV Hondius cruise ship are returning to their home countries, some with symptoms, many without. What happens to these people and what is being done to contain the spread?
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: Architecture of remembrance and hope: Daniel Libeskind at 80
item.description: Famous for designing the Jewish Museum Berlin and the Ground Zero project in New York, the architect has created dozens of memorable buildings worldwide.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Hantavirus: How it differs from COVID
item.description: In 2018-2019, the Andes virus hantavirus spread person-to-person in Argentina. Eleven people died — but it was stopped. Analysis of that outbreak shows the current one can also be stopped.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: Germany news: 1-in-6 retailers fear for future
item.description: About one-in-12 German businesses worry about survival, with twice as many retailers jittery. Meanwhile in Stuttgart, five pro-Palestinian activists face trial over attacking an Israeli defense company site. Follow DW.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Ukraine: EU sanctions Russians over 'systematic unlawful deportation' of children
item.description: The EU is sanctioning 16 individuals and seven entities over alleged child abductions. The UK also issued fresh sanctions, and Germany's defense minister visited Kyiv amid a shaky ceasefire. DW has the latest.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer 'not walking away' after local election mauling
item.description: Keir Starmer has said he takes "responsibility" for Labour's poor election performance, but also for putting matters right again. "I know I have my doubters and I know I need to prove them wrong, and I will," he said.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Endemic, epidemic, pandemic: What's the difference?
item.description: The Andes hantavirus outbreak shows how easily disease labels are misunderstood — and why they describe patterns of spread, not how dangerous a virus is.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: Somali piracy disrupts global shipping and trade routes
item.description: As ships reroute around Africa to avoid Middle East conflict zones, piracy off Somalia is back. The resurgence is driving up insurance, transit times and security costs, putting new pressure on global supply chains.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Germany's businesses frustrated by economic decline
item.description: No one had higher hopes for Friedrich Merz's chancellorship than the German business community. Now, however, early euphoria has given way to bitter disappointment.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Why is Hollywood missing from Cannes?
item.description: There are no US blockbusters premiering at the Cannes Film Festival this year. So who's snubbing who?
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Could German ex-leader really negotiate Ukraine peace?
item.description: Former German chancellor Gerhard Schröder is a controversial — and some critics would even say, unserious — choice as a negotiator on the Ukraine war. He's long been criticized for being too close to Russia.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Germany: 'Ulm 5' on trial after attack on Israeli arms firm
item.description: Five activists have gone on trial in Germany following a break-in at the Israeli arms company Elbit Systems in Ulm. Their lawyers intend to argue they were trying to obstruct a genocide.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: 'No comment' after Schröder named by Putin for Ukraine talks
item.description: Russian President Vladimir Putin suggested that the former German chancellor serve as a mediator in Russia-Ukraine peace talks.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Berlin: Fighting the city's young far-right scene
item.description: A recent police raid on Germany's neo-Nazi scene has revealed how alarmingly young some of its members are. But those targeted by this hatred are fighting back.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Data centers: Critical tech infrastructure under threat
item.description: Data centers are energy-intensive engines of growth, the backbone and hub of digitalization. Thousands of them are being built all around the world. The Iran war has shown how vulnerable societies are as a result.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Antisemitism in Germany, and the CDU's search for answers
item.description: German Chancellor Friedrich Merz's CDU party has been struggling to find the right words on how it intends to protect Jewish life in the capital. A recent declaration does not go far beyond calling for "a clear stance."
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Greek minister says mystery drone from a 'foreign state'
item.description: A Greek military source said the robot, found this week off the island of Lefkada, may have been Ukrainian. Kyiv has admitted using drones to target Russian oil tankers in the Mediterranean.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Putin says he thinks Ukraine war is 'coming to an end'
item.description: The Russian president made the remarks as Moscow and Kyiv observe a three-day ceasefire in their four-year conflict.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: New film portrays Thomas and Erika Mann — with an oddly twisted premise
item.description: Premiering at Cannes and starring Sandra Hüller, Pawel Pawlikowski's "Fatherland" is set during the Cold War — a complex period for the prominent anti-Nazi intellectuals.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Eurovision 2026: Politics collide with a blockbuster show
item.description: Controversy over Israel's participation and boycotts unprecedented in scale are already weighing on the Eurovision Song Contest before it even kicks off in Vienna.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Germany's sugar tax sparks 'nanny state' debate
item.description: Friedrich Merz's government has agreed to introduce a sugar levy on soft drinks as part of its health care reforms. Critics have called it disproportionate interference, but dozens of countries already have such a levy.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Trump announces 3-day ceasefire between Ukraine and Russia
item.description: The US president said both sides had agreed to halt fighting from May 9 to 11 and to exchange 1,000 prisoners each. Both Ukraine and Russia confirmed the ceasefire and prisoner exchange. Follow DW for more.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: How real is a coup threat against Russia's president?
item.description: Increased security around Vladimir Putin and a scaling back of public appearances have given rise to suspicions of a coup or assassination attempt. Yet some observers remain skeptical.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Hungary: New govt intent on initiating swift systemic change
item.description: Peter Magyar and his government are determined to push ahead with change at full throttle. But even before the cabinet is sworn in, there has been a "resignation." And questions remain about Magyar's Ukraine policy.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Why Russia has scaled back May 9 Victory Parade
item.description: This year's May 9 military parades in Moscow will be much more modest and have been canceled altogether in other parts of Russia. The Kremlin has blamed Ukraine.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Germany's far-right AfD benefits from discontent with Merz
item.description: Never before has a German government been this unpopular after only one year in office as that of Chancellor Friedrich Merz. The far-right AfD is capitalizing on this.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Celebrated broadcaster and naturalist David Attenborough turns 100
item.description: The still-sprightly centenarian has been knighted twice, nominated for the 2022 Nobel Peace Prize, and is perhaps the world's most recognizable nature lover.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: "We've lived with them for 80 years": Inside a German town that US troops could soon leave after Donald Trump's withdrawal threat
item.description: If US President Donald Trump is serious, 5,000 US soldiers could be withdrawn from Germany. This would include the Stryker Brigade in Vilseck. The town is in shock.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: World Cup 'prices will drop' but too late for traveling fans
item.description: Among many firsts at World Cup 2026, FIFA has tried its hand at dynamic pricing and acting as a secondary seller. The tactic has seen a ticket listed for over $2 million, but many games seem undersold. What's going on?
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Austria: Police report 3 dead in shooting in Linz, say no further danger to public
item.description: Police in Austria report that three people have died after gunfire was heard in the city. Local media reported a large police presence in the north of the city. Police also said there was no ongoing threat to the public.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Why the EU sees Chinese solar tech as a major security risk
item.description: Europe's solar boom is powered by Chinese technology. But experts warn Chinese-made tech could threaten the continent's safety and even create blackouts. Now, Brussels is aiming to reduce its dependence.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Germany news: Coalition at odds over pay rises for MPs
item.description: German conservatives want to hold on to automatic pay increases for lawmakers, while the center-left thinks they should skip it this year in light of the economic situation. DW has more.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: EU reaches tentative deal on simpler AI rules, plans ban on 'nudifier' apps
item.description: In a provisional deal, the European Union has agreed on a ban on the use of AI for creating pornographic deepfakes and sexualized imagery. It has also agreed on amending certain rules within the EU's AI Act.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: 'Dark patterns': Investigating online manipulation
item.description: Ireland's media regulator is investigating Facebook and Instagram on suspicion that so-called dark patterns are used to manipulate users.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Bayern Munich exit Champions League in semifinal to PSG
item.description: Holders Paris Saint-Germain are one game away from retaining the Champions League after knocking out German champions Bayern Munich. Bayern can still complete the domestic double but again miss out on the big one.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Germany cracks down on neo-Nazi networks
item.description: Police and prosecutors have launched a nationwide crackdown on young right-wing extremists. They are alleged to have formed criminal organizations and attacked members of the left-wing scene.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: COVID pioneer BioNTech: The fall of Germany's pandemic hero
item.description: BioNTech, once celebrated for its COVID-19 vaccines, is grappling with falling demand, restructuring and the departure of its founders. Can its ambitious cancer trials turn it from a one-hit wonder into a biotech giant?
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Venice Biennale in crisis: The controversies explained
item.description: Following the resignation of the jury over Russia's and Israel's disputed participation, the traditional Golden Lion will not be awarded this year.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Hungary's new government pushes for euro by 2030
item.description: Hungary's new leadership wants the country to adopt the euro by the end of the decade while repairing strained ties with Brussels. But with a weak economy and tight deadlines, experts warn the path will be steep.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: What the Strait of Hormuz fuel crisis means for air travel
item.description: Does travel insurance cover flight cancellations caused by the Iran war? Travelers could face major disruption as the jet fuel crisis threatens summer flights.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: How Rosalia became a global pop force
item.description: From a small town in Spain to sold-out global arenas, Spanish singer Rosalia has built her career with precision rather than luck. How did she do it?
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Germany: Who is CDU parliamentary leader Jens Spahn?
item.description: CDU firebrand, ex-health minister, and leader of his party's parliamentary group, Jens Spahn has been dogged by controversy. But his ambition is undimmed.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Germany prepares for mission in the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: The German minesweeper Fulda has set course for the Mediterranean. The German armed forces may assist in clearing mines in the Strait of Hormuz — although only under certain conditions.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Germany: BioNTech shutting down COVID vaccine production entirely, 1,860 jobs at risk
item.description: The German company that pioneered one of the first mRNA coronavirus vaccines is halting production, handing the reins to partner Pfizer. BioNTech is restructuring to focus on research and illnesses like cancer.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Germany: Difficult first year for Chancellor Friedrich Merz
item.description: A year after taking office, Germany's federal leadership is struggling with the drudgery of coalition governance. Whenever there are problems, the coalition partners are blamed. Or US President Donald Trump.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: What is hantavirus?
item.description: Three people have died on a cruise ship in the Atlantic, reportedly from hantavirus. The dangerous Andes strain has been confirmed in two cases. What are the symptoms, and how is it transmitted?
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: The sea that is vanishing in real time
item.description: The world's largest inland body of water is retreating at an alarming speed. From stranded buildings to vanishing habitats, scientists warn the Caspian Sea may be approaching a tipping point.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: From India to Iran: How Hitler redefined 'Aryan' for the Nazis
item.description: According to Nazi ideology, an ideal "Aryan" was blond, blue-eyed with athletic features. The term is still tied to Nazi Germany, but its origin lies elsewhere.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Dubai's image as a financial hub faces its biggest test yet
item.description: Amid the smoldering Iran war, wealthy elites are quietly shifting capital and other assets out of Dubai and heading to Singapore and Switzerland. Just how resilient is the emirate's safe-haven reputation?
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Why Saudi Arabia is withdrawing from sport
item.description: There are few major sports left that are not supported by investment from Saudi Arabia. But with the kingdom pulling out of golf and canceling planned events and contracts elsewhere, is the Saudi sports era over?
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Georg Baselitz, artist renowned for his upside-down paintings, dies
item.description: He was thrown out of art school at the age of 18 because of his love for Picasso. The German artist who never lost his rebellious streak has died at the age of 88.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Angine de Poitrine: A masked rock band conquers the internet
item.description: From underground experiment to sold-out international concerts within days — as the French-Canadian band Angine de Poitrine starts touring the world, we take a look at the viral phenomenon.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Women's Champions League: Bayern fall to Barcelona in semis
item.description: Bayern Munich have come up short in their bid to win the Women's Champions League for the first time after losing to Barcelona in the semifinals.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Millions sway as free Shakira concert lights up Copacabana
item.description: The Colombian superstar delivers one of the biggest shows of her career, as fans flock from Brazil and beyond for her "Women No Longer Cry" world tour.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Alex Zanardi, F1 driver and multiple CART and Paralympic champion, dies at 59
item.description: Italian race driver turned Paralympian Alex Zanardi has died aged 59. The former Formula 1 driver and two-time CART champion lost his legs in a horrific crash at the peak of his powers, but his drive to compete endured.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Gender investment gap: Are women catching up with men?
item.description: Online brokers are tapping into the potential of female investors. Tailor-made products and branding campaigns are on the rise. Will this help bridge the gap between women and men when it comes to investing?
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Iran's Vafaei playing for more than snooker glory
item.description: After years lost to visa battles, Hossein Vafaei is finally delivering on his early promise as a snooker prodigy. He's hoping to inspire people back home amid the turmoil in Iran.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Study busts myth: German 'spring fatigue' doesn't exist
item.description: Do we really feel more tired in spring? Researchers in Switzerland say, "there's no such thing as spring fatigue." But it is a deep cultural phenomenon in German-speaking countries.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: How widespread is global fatigue with the US?
item.description: The global image of the US has taken a blow, as many people are increasingly fed up with the constant stream of controversies surrounding the Trump administration.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: A new climate club, minus the fossil fuel lobby
item.description: Frustrated by blocking coalitions, 50-plus nations at the inaugural talks on exiting fossil fuels made one thing clear: it's all about how — not whether — to phase out coal, oil and gas.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Will EU bring in a windfall tax on oil companies?
item.description: Five countries, including Germany, want the EU to bring in a so-called windfall tax to target energy companies benefiting from the price crisis. It can be done, but it's complex.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Why UAE's OPEC exit is a blow to Saudi Arabia
item.description: The United Arab Emirates is leaving OPEC to pump more oil on its own terms. The break strips Saudi Arabia of a key partner and adds to growing uncertainty over the cartel's future.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Football: Afghan women's team recognized in blow to Taliban
item.description: Banned from playing football and sent into exile, Afghanistan's women's team has had to fight to regain international status. That newly awarded recognition is a "hard slap to the face" of the Taliban, a player told DW.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: 2026 Goethe Medal: Meet the recipients
item.description: Though they work in different fields and countries, what unites them is their focus on a Europe in transition: Arvo Pärt, Anita Raja and Prodromos Tsinikoris have been awarded the 2026 Goethe Medal.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: 'Rose' and the piece of fabric that embodies gender politics
item.description: As the film starring Sandra Hüller is released in Germany, DW talked to director Markus Schleinzer about why women passing as men inspired his historical drama, and why it's a current topic.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Extreme weather and green energy on the rise in Europe
item.description: Europe's climate extremes have hit new highs — but renewables are now supplying nearly half of the continent's electricity.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Germany's entrepreneurs betting on post-Maduro Venezuela
item.description: Venezuela's economy is opening sectors to foreign firms, and that could pay off for the few German companies that have stuck it out through years of decline.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: What Renault's new EV reveals about the global auto industry
item.description: French carmaker Renault no longer sells its brand in the Chinese market, but it's not afraid to design and develop them there. It's just one more sign of big shifts in the global auto industry.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Bundesliga games abroad unlikely, but for how long?
item.description: New proposals from FIFA will allow domestic leagues to host one game abroad a season. As a result, Europe's football leagues could face a big change sooner rather than later.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: 'Marine unicorns' aren't loving Arctic noise
item.description: Narwhals are fleeing Canada's far north. Researchers suspect a link to noise pollution from increasing ship traffic.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Will the Iran war end Strait of Hormuz oil supremacy?
item.description: The conflict has brutally exposed the energy market's Achilles' heel. While global powers China, India and the EU push renewables, Gulf leaders are advancing plans for new bypass pipelines to safeguard their oil empires.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Sawe smashes 2-hour mark setting record at London Marathon
item.description: Kenya's Sabastian Sawe has made history in London as the first man to run a marathon in under two hours. He beat the world record by more than a minute.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Queer as folk: The gay scandal that shook the German Empire
item.description: Germany's last emperor, a gilded porcelain vase and a general who had a heart attack in a pink tutu: The Eulenburg Affair shook the German Empire and Europe to its core and paved the way for the downfall of the monarchy.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: The silent power of memes
item.description: They are funny, provocative and sometimes tasteless. But memes are also powerful political tool, shaping our perception — usually without us noticing.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: States gather in a new push to ditch coal, oil and gas
item.description: At an unprecedented meeting in Colombia, delegates from more than 50 countries want to chart a practical path to wean the world off fossil fuels. Can they succeed where the UN climate talks have failed?
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Meta, Microsoft purge jobs amid AI build-up
item.description: Facebook's parent company said it would slash 10% of its workforce, while Microsoft is offering an early retirement scheme. The cuts come as both tech giants make massive investments in AI.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: The country that's turning to solar during war
item.description: Nuclear disaster is not just an abstract concept in Ukraine. It is both history and an ongoing threat that is pushing communities to turn to the sun for energy.
item.dc:subject: Climate

item.title: A book lover's dream holiday in Scotland
item.description: As UNESCO celebrates World Book Day on April 23, we visit a Scottish coastal town that was saved by books.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Bayern Munich on the brink of historic 'double treble'
item.description: With Bundesliga titles in the bag and cup finals to come, Bayern's men and women have a chance to achieve something unique in Europe. The two teams dominate in Germany and appear united in their Champions League aims.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Russia to block Kazakh oil flows to Germany via key pipeline
item.description: Germany has confirmed that Russia will block flows of Kazakh oil to a vital refinery that supplies much of Berlin's energy needs from May 1.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: The rise and fall of East Germany's Palace of the Republic
item.description: From 1976 through 1990, the building in East Berlin served as a symbol of the communist state's power. The "People's Palace" was controversially destroyed — and yet remains present.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Could Iran war trigger bigger trade crisis than COVID?
item.description: As the Iran war simmers, trade experts ask whether it could reshape supply chains more deeply than COVID-19, forcing companies to rethink strategies to counter further geopolitical shocks.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Iran war: Jet fuel crisis deepens as Lufthansa cuts flights
item.description: The impact of jet fuel shortages because of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is starting to burn. Lufthansa has canceled 20,000 flights and experts warn more cancellations and price hikes are on the way.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: How grievance turns to violence in a mass shooter's mind
item.description: Attacks like last week's school shooting in Turkey can seem to come out of nowhere, but they rarely do. Instead, a buildup of grievance and fixation often precedes the the moment violent thoughts become violent acts.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: Mars: NASA's Curiosity rover finds new organic matter in Gale Crater
item.description: NASA's Curiosity rover has identified more organic compounds as scientists investigate any potential ancient life signs on Mars. It was exploring the massive Gale Crater also believed to be a now-dry lake.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Apple switches CEO: What it means for the tech giant
item.description: Apple's top hardware engineer John Ternus will lead the $4 trillion iPhone maker, as Tim Cook steps back. Will Apple chase rivals for the smartest intelligence, or deliver the best personal AI experience?
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Can data centers be green?
item.description: Renewable energy is increasingly being used to supply power-hungry data centers. As the sector swells, much of the electricity demand is being met by polluting fossil fuels.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Tim Cook to step down as Apple CEO, John Ternus to take over
item.description: John Ternus, who has long focused ​on design and ‌products at Apple, will lead the iPhone maker as it gears up for an industry change spurred by artificial intelligence.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Bundesliga debut by female coach marred by misogyny
item.description: Marie-Louise Eta's historic debut as a head coach in the men's Bundesliga ended in defeat. While her appointment is a sign of progress, the reactions to it leave no doubt about how much is still to be done.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: China's chip ambitions shake up global tech industry
item.description: US curbs on advanced chips pushed China to focus on its own semiconductor ecosystem. And while it trails at the very cutting edge, China's "good‑enough" technology is fast powering much of the global economy.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Mines in the Strait of Hormuz: How dangerous are they?
item.description: Iran says it has placed mines in the Straight of Hormuz to discourage independent traffic. How dangerous are naval mines, and what can be done to clear them?
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: Climate change impacts India's harvest festivals
item.description: As many Indians celebrate spring festivals, climate change has become an unwanted guest at the table. What does this mean for harvests, water and rural life?
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Hannover Messe 2026: Brazilian flair awaits visitors
item.description: With war in Iran on hold, the world's largest industrial trade fair opens in Hannover. And Brazil, this year's partner country, is pulling out all stops to convince visitors it's a reliable technology leader.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: The German who recovered from a stroke to seal Olympic first
item.description: Kathrin Marchand was an elite rower for years before becoming a doctor. But a stroke at the age of 30 changed everything. Despite the challenges, she has now achieved something no other Olympian ever has.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Record fuel prices: How are governments responding?
item.description: In Germany, a liter of diesel costs up to 40% more than before the Iran war and other countries have seen even bigger price jumps. What are governments around the world doing to combat record fuel prices?
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Teen discovers first ancient Greek artifact found in Berlin
item.description: A 13-year-old stumbled upon a coin from ancient Troy — an extremely rare find in Germany. Experts say it may offer new clues about little-known links between ancient Greece and northern Europe.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Chess: Uzbekistan's new star shows Asia's continued rise
item.description: Javokhir Sindarov of Uzbekistan has earned the right to challenge India's Dommaraju Gukesh for the world title. Both men are under 21 and from Asia, underlining an ongoing demographic shift in top level chess.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Nueva Germania: The failed 'Aryan project' in Paraguay
item.description: A new home for the "Aryan race." That was what German emigrants envisioned when they founded Nueva Germania in Paraguay in 1886. It failed back then but a version of the town still exists today.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Anatomy of the clitoris: 3D images reveal its nerve system
item.description: New 3D images reveal the clitoris’s complex nerve network, highlighting long‑standing gaps in medical understanding of the female body.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: World Cup 2026: Fans with disabilities 'excluded'
item.description: Complaints that the 2026 World Cup, hosted largely by the USA, is a rip off have been widespread and constant. But it's not just the prices that are making it feel impossible for football fans with disabilities.
item.dc:subject: Sports
